data.title: Live updates: Senate passes massive government funding package
data.description:
The Senate voted overwhelmingly Friday night to pass a major funding package, but the legislation won’t become law before parts of the government shut down at midnight.

In a 71-29 vote, the Senate approved the package consisting of five regular appropriations bills and a two-week stopgap measure for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). The legislation will now head to the House, which could vote on it Monday.

The upper chamber had hit a snag in its deal-making when Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) placed a hold on the package late Thursday night.

But he relaxed his stance on Friday morning, allowing for a vote in exchange for a later vote on legislation to the conduct of state and local officials who refuse to cooperate with federal immigration enforcement operations.

Earlier Friday, President Trump announced his choice to succeed Jerome Powell as chair of the Federal Reserve, naming Kevin Warsh to the role.

“I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best,” Trump said of Warsh, a former Fed governor who was runner-up to Powell for chair in 2017.

The Justice Department on Friday said it had fulfilled its congressional mandate in making public the remaining 3 million documents in the Jeffrey Epstein files.

Follow live here.

data.title: Sheriff: ICE must be 'transparent' with any Super Bowl operations
data.description:
A California sheriff on Thursday called on Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to be “transparent” with local authorities about any possible federal immigration operations around Levi’s Stadium during Super Bowl LX.

“So I urge our federal partners: if there is something that you’re planning to do in our community, please be transparent and reach out to your local officials so we can work as best we can,” Santa Clara County Sheriff Robert Jonsen said. “It was mentioned, we are not going to change our policies or protocols. We will not be working or supporting ICE immigration enforcement.”

“But I want you to again remember [that] if you see us out there, we’re there to help you, and we will be there,” Jonsen continued. “So don’t hesitate to contact somebody wearing a uniform — as these events unfold if they’re walking around and trying to engage — and make sure that you remain safe.”

The sheriff said there was a “high probability” that those attending the game will see people in uniform, but he noted that if officers are “not masked, if they’re wearing tan and green or blue or black, trust me, they’re there for your protection.”

“If they’re masked and they’re trying to hide their identity, then someone hasn’t communicated with us,” Jonsen said.

Messaging from the Trump administration about whether ICE will be present during the game has been mixed. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin told KQED, a San Francisco-based news outlet affiliated with PBS and NPR, that the department is not disclosing any future operations.

“Super Bowl security will entail a whole-of-government response conducted in line with the U.S. Constitution,” she said in a statement. “Those who are here legally and are not breaking other laws have nothing to fear.”

The Hill has reached out to DHS to confirm if ICE will be present during the game.

In October, DHS adviser Corey Lewandowski said on “The Benny Show” that ICE will be at the game, saying it was a “directive from the president.”

“We will find you. We will apprehend you. We will put you in a detention facility, and we will deport you,” Lewandowski said. “So know that that is a very real situation under this administration, which is completely contrary to what how it used to be.”

Shortly after, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said there was “no tangible plan” for ICE to be at the Super Bowl.

Lewandowski’s remarks came after the NFL announced that artist Bad Bunny will perform during the game’s halftime show. He slammed the NFL’s choice, claiming the league “decided to pick somebody who just seems to hate America so much to represent them at the halftime game.”

President Trump told the New York Post that he will not attend the game, saying it is “just too far away.”

Super Bowl LX is scheduled for Feb. 8 and will feature the Seattle Seahawks in a rematch with the six-time champion New England Patriots. The Patriots defeated the Seahawks in the 2015 Super Bowl.

data.title: Don Lemon after release: 'I will not stop now, I will not stop ever'
data.description:
Former CNN host Don Lemon, released a day after federal officers arrested him in Los Angeles, defended his career in journalism on Friday, saying he will never stop reporting.

U.S. District Judge Patricia Donahue ordered Lemon to be released on no money bond, according to The Hollywood Reporter. Lemon was accused of disturbing worshippers in a Minnesota church by disrupting their First Amendment right to practice their faith.

Outside a downtown Los Angeles courthouse, Lemon thanked people supporting him and defended his work.

“I will not stop now. I will not stop ever,” Lemon said. “In fact, there is no more important time but right now, this very moment, for a free and independent media that shines a light on truth and holds those in power accountable.”

Lemon said the Department of Justice (DOJ) sent federal officers to arrest him “for something I’ve been doing for the last 30 years, and that is covering the news.”

“The First Amendment of the Constitution protects that work for me and for countless other journalists who do what I do,” he continued. “I stand with all of them and I will not be silenced. I look forward to my day in court.”

U.S. Attorney Alexander Robbins accused Lemon, during his court appearance, of joining a “mob that terrorized people in a place of worship,” The Hollywood Reporter wrote. Lemon is prohibited from speaking to any witnesses, victims or defendants in the case and must ask permission to leave the country.

The Trump administration sought to charge the independent journalist and seven others in connection with a protest at a St. Paul church where the pastor, David Easterwood, was believed by protesters to be the head of Minnesota field office for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

Attorney General Pam Bondi on Friday claimed Lemon was arrested in connection with “a coordinated attack on Cities Church in St. Paul, Minnesota.” Though some administration officials claimed a federal grand jury indicted Lemon, such an indictment has not been unsealed.

Lemon was previously a prime-time host on CNN for several years before he was fired from the network in 2023. He now hosts “The Don Lemon Show” on YouTube and Substack as an independent reporter.

The journalist received support from current and former officials following his arrest. Former Vice President Kamala Harris said Lemon’s arrest “is another affront to our rights and freedoms and should alarm and enrage us.”

“ICE is occupying communities and shooting Americans,” Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) said on the social platform X. “Now, former CNN anchor Don Lemon has been arrested for covering what ICE is doing. Arresting journalists is what happens in tin-pot dictatorships. We must fight back against authoritarianism.”

data.title: Senate passes massive government funding package, sending it to House
data.description:
The Senate voted overwhelmingly Friday to pass a major funding package consisting of five regular appropriations bills and a two-week stopgap measure for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) but the legislation won’t become law before parts of the government shut down at midnight.

The 71-29 vote caps a week of Senate drama after a Border Patrol agent shot and killed 37-year-old nurse Alex Pretti in Minneapolis on Saturday, sparking a public backlash against the tactics of federal immigration enforcement officers and pushing Democrats to oppose the DHS funding bill that was part of a six-bill funding package.

Democrats vowed to block the entire funding package that had been carefully negotiated over months between Republican and Democratic members of the Appropriations Committee, putting Congress on course for what some lawmakers feared would be another prolonged government shutdown.

But senators managed to save the funding package by negotiating a last-minute deal with the White House to split off the Homeland Security appropriations bill from the five other spending measures funding the departments of Defense, Health and Human Services, Transportation and State, among others.

While the legislation passed the Senate before government funding expires at midnight, it still needs to pass the House, which is slated to return on Monday, before going to President Trump’s desk for a signature.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) told House Republicans in a Friday afternoon conference call that he hopes to quickly pass the bill under a fast-track process known as suspension of the rules, which allows him to bypass the difficult task of getting Republicans unified behind a rule to pass the package under regular order.

The suspension process requires a two-thirds vote to suspend the rules and pass legislation.

The Office of Management and Budget says it will issue an order directing a partial shutdown of government because of the lapse in funding.

The partial shutdown will affect a broad swath of the federal government, including the Department of Defense.

But senators feared only days ago that they could be headed for another prolonged shutdown after Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) initially balked at Democrats’ demands to pull the full-year Homeland Security appropriations bill out of a six-bill government funding package.

A turning point in the standoff over the funding package came when Trump signaled to Thune and Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) that he would support unbundling it.

Almost every member of the Senate Democratic caucus, including Democrats who bucked their leadership and voted to keep the government open in the fall, had vowed to block the massive bill if it included full-year Homeland Security funding.

Democrats hailed passage of the amended legislation Friday as a major victory and said they would demand major reforms to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Customs and Border Protection (CBP) over the next two weeks when Senate and House negotiators try to reach a deal to avoid a Homeland Security shutdown in mid-February.

“Today, the Senate voted to pass five funding bills that invest in working families and reject President Trump’s catastrophic cuts—while splitting off the DHS funding bill and forcing Congress to take action to rein in ICE and CBP,” Sen. Patty Murray (D-Wash.), the vice chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee, said in a statement.

Democrats were elated to get five of the regular appropriations bills over the finish line, pointing to a number of victories in the bill, such as a rejection of President Trump’s push to cut the maximum Pell Grant by more than $1000.

The bill also rejected Trump’s plans to cut rental assistance and National Institutes of Health funding.  

It provides $20 million in additional spending for the Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program, $85 million more for the Child Care and Development Block Grant and $85 million more for Head Start education, which Democrats touted as big wins.

But some conservative Republican senators were furious that the Homeland Security bill was left out of the package after Democrats had signed off on it only weeks ago.

“I believe this is a horrible bill. I can’t believe we’re not funding ICE,” said Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.). “I don’t believe in two weeks it’s going to get funded.”

Democrats insisted on Wednesday that they would only support a two-week funding stopgap for the Department of Homeland Security. Democrats from Minnesota such as Sen. Tina Smith argued that changes need to be made immediately to rein in the aggressive tactics of ICE and Border Patrol officers who are arresting migrants in Minneapolis — and who had killed two U.S. citizens in recent weeks.

Thune and other Republicans initially resisted such a short funding stopgap, arguing that it would take nearly two weeks just to process another full-year appropriations bill in both the Senate and House. Republicans floated a four- or six-week funding extension, instead.

But Democrats held firm on the issue and won another victory.

The funding package also includes legislation to repeal a law enacted in the fall to allow senators whose phone records were secretly subpoenaed by special counsel Jack Smith to sue the Department of Justice for hundreds of thousands of dollars.

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), who was one of eight GOP senators caught up in the Justice Department’s “Arctic Frost” investigation, was furious that the bill included the House-passed provision blocking his right to sue the government.

Graham put a hold on the package Thursday evening, delaying floor action until Friday.

“I fixed the problem that people had. I’m not going to ignore what happened. If you were abused, you think you were abused, your phone records were illegally seized — you should have your day in court,” he told reporters Thursday. “Every senator should want to make sure this never happens again.”

Graham later dropped his hold on Friday afternoon after securing a promise from Thune to vote on a later date on two proposals: one to criminalize the actions of state and local officials who do not cooperate with federal immigration enforcement operations and a second to give individuals who are not members of Congress a private cause of action to sue the Department of Justice if they were targeted by the special counsel’s investigations of Trump.

data.title: Lady Gaga: Heart aches for families 'mercilessly targeted by ICE'
data.description:
Lady Gaga on Thursday said her heart aches for families in the U.S. “mercilessly targeted” by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) amid President Trump’s immigration crackdown and growing tensions with Minnesota local and state officials over immigration operations.

“In a couple of days, I’m going to be heading home and my heart is aching thinking about the people, the children, the families all over America who are being mercilessly targeted by ICE,” Gaga said while on her “Mayhem Ball” tour in Tokyo. “I’m thinking about all of their pain and how their lives are being destroyed right in front of us.

“I’m also thinking about Minnesota and everyone back at home who is living in so much fear and searching for answers on what we all should do,” she added. “When entire communities lose their sense of safety and belonging, it breaks something in all of us. I hope that you’ll all stand with us tonight.”

Gaga is one of many celebrities, including singers Billie Eilish and Katy Perry, who have condemned ICE and similar agencies carrying out federal immigration operations. Tensions grew after two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, were killed during some of these operations in Minneapolis.

“The Mandalorian” actor Pedro Pascal on Instagram called for investigations into “what happened” with Good and Pretti’s deaths. Pascal also supported calls for a “national shutdown” on Friday, where people do not go to work or school and not go shopping in protest against ICE and in response to Good and Pretti’s deaths.

Pascal’s post also showed drawings of Good and Pretti above the words “Pretti Good reason for a national strike.”

Actor and longtime activist Jane Fonda said federal officers “are kidnapping people” and “illegally deporting American citizens.”

“They’re shooting people,” she said in an appearance on “The Late Show” with Stephen Colbert. “They’re blinding people, all kinds of really, really bad things are happening. And it’s not a question of right or left. I don’t care what party you belong to, it’s a question of right or wrong. And I think lines are being crossed, and it’s enough. We’ve had enough. We are the land of the brave.”

On Thursday night, Trump dismissed the notion that he will pull back federal officers from Minnesota, despite weeks of calls to do so from local and state elected officials. The president said there will be withdrawal, “not at all.”

“We keep our country safe,” Trump said at the “Melania” film premiere at the Kennedy Center. “We’ll do whatever we can to keep our country safe.”

data.title: Crockett, Talarico locked in dead heat in Texas Senate primary: Poll
data.description:
The top two Democratic candidates in Texas’s Senate race are neck and neck in a new poll. 

A Texas Public Opinion Research poll surveyed likely Democratic primary voters ahead of the state party’s upcoming primary elections on March 3. 

In response to a question about whom they would cast their ballots for if the primary were held today, 38 percent of respondents said they would vote for U.S. Rep. Jasmine Crockett, while 37 percent supported state Rep. James Talarico. 

The two candidates’ platforms represent a greater divide within the Democratic Party over how best to counter a GOP-controlled federal government. 

Crockett, a political firebrand, is known as a fierce Democratic advocate and for her combative approach against Republican legislators on the House floor. Talarico, a former minister and schoolteacher, has branded himself as a more moderate choice, focused on bridging party divides. 

Of the voters supporting Crockett, 22 percent said their primary reason for backing the congresswoman was “people first advocacy & fairness/justice.” Among Talarico’s supporters, 32 percent said they valued “character, integrity & relatability.”

Additionally, over half of respondents ranked affordability and cost of living as one of their top concerns in this election, and 38 percent of voters ranked addressing health care access and costs as a top issue. 

The winner of the Democratic primary will go up against the GOP candidate in the general election on Nov. 3. 

Sitting Sen. John Cornyn (R) is being challenged by several Republicans, including Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton. Trump has not yet backed a candidate in the race. 

Texas has not had a Democratic senator since 1993. In 2020, Cornyn beat his Democratic opponent by nearly 10 percentage points. 

The poll, which had a margin of error of plus or minus 3.7 percentage points, collected responses from 1,290 likely Democratic primary voters between Jan. 14-21.

data.title: Kamala Harris: Don Lemon arrest 'an affront to our rights and freedoms'
data.description:
Former Vice President and Democratic presidential nominee Kamala Harris is blasting President Trump’s administration over the arrest of journalist Don Lemon and several other independent media members in connection with a protest at a church in Minnesota earlier this month.

“Today, Donald Trump and his administration are once again trampling on our rights and our freedoms. Journalists Don Lemon and Georgia Fort were fulfilling their duty to the American people to report and inform, and they have been arrested for it,” Harris wrote in a statement posted on social media. “Donald Trump continues to consolidate power and show a flagrant disregard for the rule of law. This arrest is another affront to our rights and freedoms and should alarm and enrage us.”

Lemon was arrested Friday in Los Angeles on charges in connection with the demonstration, which he and his producers filmed at a St. Paul church where anti-ICE protesters were disrupting a service because they believed the pastor there was a member of immigration enforcement.

A federal magistrate initially declined to take up charges against Lemon, who has argued he was not participating in the demonstration but rather covering the confrontation as a journalist.

A number of press freedom advocates and prominent Democrats have slammed the Department of Justice over the pundit’s arrest, arguing his detainment is the latest example of the Trump administration cracking down on unfavorable press coverage and commentary.

A former longtime anchor at CNN, Lemon launched a YouTube and social media show after being fired by the network, and has used that platform to criticize Trump and his allies.

CNN in a statement called Lemon’s arrest “unacceptable,” and noted “the Department of Justice already failed twice to get an arrest warrant for Don and several other journalists in Minnesota, where a chief judge of the Minnesota Federal District Court found there was ’no evidence’ that there was any criminal behavior involved in their work.”

Attorney General Pam Bondi and the White House, however, have argued Lemon was actively participating in a protest that violated the religious freedom rights of those at the church that day.

“Make no mistake under president Trump’s leadership, you have the right to worship freely and safely,” Bondi said in a video posted to her own social media. “And if I haven’t been clear already, if you violate that sacred right, we are coming after you.”

data.title: Health care costs top concern in new poll
data.description:
Americans are more worried about affording health care than any other household necessity, according to a Kaiser Family Foundation poll published Thursday. 

The poll, conducted with 1,426 adults between Jan. 13 and 20, found that two-thirds of respondents felt more anxious about paying for health care than for the most basic household essentials including utilities, food and groceries, housing and gas. More than half of respondents reported a rise in their health insurance rates in the past year and an expectation for them to continue rising in the coming year.  

The poll, which had a margin of error of plus or minus 3 percentage points, found that one-in-five people reported their health care costs were rising faster than the prices of their food and utilities. 

The poll found relative bipartisan consistency over health care affordability with 71 percent of Democrats, 68 percent of Independents and 57 percent of Republicans reporting being either somewhat or very worried about affording health insurance as well as out-of-pocket costs associated with health care like office visits and prescription drugs. 

Concerns about the ost of health care come after the Senate stalled a House bill to extend Affordable Care Act subsidies which expired on Jan. 1. Out of pocket insurance costs were estimated to double without the funding leading to enrollment dropping by more than 1 million.

Large majorities of Democrats and Independents said Congress did the “wrong thing” by not extending the subsidies while 63 percent of Republicans say lawmakers have done the “right thing.” The majority of Democratic and Independent voters said the expiration of the tax credits will have an impact on their vote in the 2026 midterms. 

As the midterms approach, two-thirds of Democratic voters and more than four out of every ten Independent voters said health care will have a “major impact” on how they decide to vote in November. About half of Republican voters said the costs will impact their decision to vote and who to vote for. 

Democrats have a 13-point advantage over Republicans when it comes to health care issues except for prescription drug prices. The poll attributes the exception to President Trump’s continuation of the Medicare drug price negotiation program which allows the federal government to negotiate directly with drug companies on prices. In December, nine pharmaceutical companies agreed to lower their prices. 

“This represents the greatest victory for patient affordability in the history of American health care, by far,” Trump said at an Oval Office press conference in December.

Similar shares of respondents reported trust in both parties to lower prescription drug costs with 35 percent of respondents saying they trusted Democrats and 30 percent saying they trusted Republicans. 

Democrats have a slight edge compared to Republicans on health care issues among Independents but many of these voters also reported not trusting either party to lower costs, according to the poll.

Voters across the board reported low confidence in Trump, Democrats and Republicans to address overall cost of living in the U.S.

data.title: Macaulay Culkin honors on-screen mom Catherine O'Hara: 'I thought we had time'
data.description:
**Related Video Above: Notable deaths of 2026

(WJW) — On Friday, actor Macaulay Culkin honored his two-time on-screen mother Catherine O’Hara upon learning of her death.

The Associated Press confirmed O’Hara’s at the age of 71, following a short illness. She played Culkin’s somewhat forgetful mom in the holiday favorites “Home Alone” and “Home Alone 2: Lost in New York”.

Culkin, 45, took to Instagram with a heartfelt message:

Mama.
I thought we had time.

I wanted more.
I wanted to sit in a chair next to you.
I heard you.
But I had so much more to say.

I love you.

I’ll see you later.

Culkin and O’Hara stayed in touch through the years and when he was given a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame in 2023, she was one of the people who gave some words at the ceremony.

O’Hara led a long and illustrious career, starting in television in the mid 1970s. The actress was nominated for multiple Primetime Emmy Awards through the decades, winning two. Her final nomination was last year, for her recurring role in HBO’s “The Last of Us.”

She gained newfound acclaim in the 2020s as part of the popular Canadian sitcom “Schitt’s Creek,” for which she won both an Emmy and a Golden Globe Award.

O’Hara is survived by husband Bo Welch, in addition to their sons, Matthew and Luke.

data.title: Axelrod warns Democrats against campaigning on abolishing ICE
data.description:
Democratic strategist David Axelrod on Thursday warned Democratic lawmakers and candidates to avoid calling for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to be abolished on the campaign trail.

“Well, you know the problem that we’ve seen before when people said abolish the police or defund the police,” Axelrod said after he was asked by CNN’s Boris Sanchez about a recent Fox News poll stating that support for abolishing ICE has doubled since 2018 at 36 percent.

“I don’t think most people who said it believed that there should be no policing function in cities, but the implication was that there could be,” Axelrod continued. “So I don’t think Democrats want to get into that.”

He said that what Democrats want is “fundamental root-branch reform.”

“And if it means getting rid of the name ICE, which has became a very bad brand, that’s one thing,” the strategist said. “If it means that we’re just going to abandon immigration enforcement, I don’t think Democrats and Republicans would support that in large numbers.”

Axelrod noted that Americans “believe you should come into the country legally, and if you don’t, there should be some penalty for that.”

Democrats from Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (N.Y.) to Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker have called for abolishing ICE. The agency faces growing pressure amid President Trump’s crackdown on immigration and after two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, were killed in Minneapolis during federal immigration operations, though Border Patrol and Customs and Border Protection officers were involved in the shooting that killed Pretti.

Rep. Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) said on Monday that the agency “in its current form cannot exist” and “needs to be abolished.”

“But ICE in its current form cannot exist, because what they’re doing right now is they’re killing people in the street, they’re violating due process,” he told Katy Tur on MS NOW. “And [Department of Homeland Secretary] Kristi Noem has to be held accountable for that,” he said later in the interview. “And people need to understand that this is a moment we have to have courage and stand up as a country and do the right thing.”

New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D) called for ICE to be abolished because “what we see is an entity that has no interest in fulfilling its stated reason to exist.” Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) said ICE should be “torn down” and be reshaped “in the image of what people want, right? And what does that look like?”

Rep. Shri Thanedar (D-Mich.) introduced legislation to dismantle ICE earlier this month, called the “Abolish ICE Act,” saying the agency was “beyond reform.”

“We do not need this paramilitary organization’s members on our streets terrorizing U.S. citizens, terrorizing moms [and] terrorizing our children,” Thanedar said at a news conference.

But not all Democrats agree. Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) defended the agency as performing “an important job for our country” and dismissed calls of abolition as “inappropriate and outrageous.”

One Democratic operative at the center of discussions on how to handle the issue previously told The Hill that there’s a “stubborn clash of perspectives” between strategists who “over-index for how the language of the moment will age” versus grassroots organizations with “a finder on the pulse of where opinion lies.”
